Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 1

It will be recalled that the preceding Surah also began with 'Alhamdulillah' (Praise be to Allah). This clearly indicates the thematic harmony between the two Surahs. The foundation of religion rests upon Monotheism (Tawhid), and the reality of Tawhid is gratitude to Allah alone, because it is Allah Who brought the heavens and the earth into existence from non-existence, and all apparent and hidden blessings received by His servants are His gifts.

'Who made the angels messengers having wings, two or three or four.' 'Who made the angels...' serves as an appositive (badal) to '[Creator] of the heavens [and the earth]'. This mentions the specific after the general to highlight its importance, indicating that the same Allah Who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth has also brought the angels into existence for the purpose of delivering messages. The reason for specifically mentioning the angels is that in Arab mythology, angels held the highest significance. They considered them to be the beloved daughters of God and worshipped them in this capacity. Their concept was that if the angels remained pleased, everything could be achieved through their mediation. This belief completely marginalized the existence of God in their lives. Although they formally acknowledged God, the focal point of all their gratitude and worship was the idols they had crafted, according to their own assumption, in the name of the angels. Here, to refute this false assumption, it is stated that Allah alone is worthy of gratitude, Who is the One Who brings all the heavens and the earth into existence from nothingness. He has appointed the angels to the duty of delivering messages. That is, these angels are neither partners in any way in the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor do they have any share in divinity; rather, they are merely God's messengers through whom God informs His prophets of His commands. This implies that those who worshipped these messengers and couriers by giving them the status of the beloved did not recognize the stature of God, nor of these messengers, nor of themselves.

'having wings' is an adjective qualifying 'messengers'. 'Ajnihah' is the plural of 'janah'. 'Janah' refers to the arms of humans as well as the wings of birds with which they fly. Here, this word is used for the angels, making it part of the 'mutashabihat' (allegorical expressions)—meaning that its true reality is known to Allah Almighty alone. The objective here is merely to inform us of the reality that not all angels are of the same rank; rather, Allah Almighty has created them with varying degrees of power and capabilities according to His wisdom. Some have less flying power, while some have more. Some fly with the power of two wings, some with three, and some with four.

Since these are the wings of angels, their actual flying power is known only to Allah, and the mention of up to four is not restrictive. The objective here is merely to point to the difference in their ranks and stations. Therefore, if Allah Almighty has other angels whose flying power is greater than this, this verse does not negate that. In some Hadiths, the number of wings of Hazrat Jibreel (Gabriel) is mentioned to be greater than this, which shows that his flying power and reach are greater than all other angels. The objective here is merely to clarify that those foolish people who placed the angels within the ranks of divinity are unaware of the high and exalted court of Allah Almighty. Let alone being partners in divinity, even as His messengers and ambassadors, they do not all share the same rank and status; rather, the reach of some is up to a certain station, while the access of others is to another place. This reality is acknowledged in Surah As-Saffat through the tongue of Hazrat Jibreel:

'And there is not among us any except that he has a known position. And indeed, we are those who stand in rows. And indeed, we are those who exalt [Allah].' (164–166)

'He increases in creation what He wills. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent.' That is, Allah Almighty can create whatever creation He wills with whatever capabilities and powers He pleases; He is competent over all things. If He created the angels for delivering His messages, this is also a miracle of His power and wisdom. If He placed a difference in their ranks, this too is a manifestation of His power—that if He wishes to make any addition to His creation or its capabilities, He is competent to do so. Increasing or decreasing attributes and capabilities is in the hands of Allah. If He has bestowed an excess of attributes and powers upon someone, this too has defined limits. Based on this, neither is it permissible for anyone to claim to be a partner in God's divinity, nor is it permissible for others to associate them in His divinity.

Ayah 2

This is presented as a proof of why God alone is worthy of gratitude. It states that whatever door of favor and mercy Allah wishes to open for you, He can open it, and no one can withhold His favor and mercy. Similarly, if He wishes to deprive you of any mercy, no one has the power to make you benefit from it. This same theme is expressed in Surah Az-Zumar as follows:

'Say, "Then have you considered what you invoke besides Allah? If Allah intended me harm, are they removers of His harm; or if He intended me mercy, are they withholders of His mercy?"' (Az-Zumar: 38)

The objective is that when the actual reality is such, your worship of the angels and Jinn, considering them beneficial or harmful, is sheer foolishness. Benefit and harm are entirely in the control of God; therefore, He alone is the sole deserving recipient of gratitude and worship.

In the verse, for the same thing (mercy/rahmah), the pronoun used first is feminine, and then it is masculine. The reason for this is that in one place, the literal word is considered, and in the other, the meaning is considered. Several examples of this have passed earlier in this book.

'after Him': meaning after His withholding. Below in this very Surah, He states: 'And if they should cease, no one could hold them after Him' (41).

'and He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise': That is, the true dominant and powerful is He alone. He opens and He withholds, and this opening and withholding occurs entirely under the requirements of His wisdom, because He is also Wise.

Ayah 3

The theme that was stated in principle in the preceding verses is now addressed to the people in a style of final establishment of proof (itmam al-hujjah), saying: 'O people, remember and reflect upon every single blessing of Allah that you possess.' The word 'ni'mah' (blessing) is used here in a generic sense. To make them reflect, the issue was placed before them in the form of a question: this provision that you receive from the heavens and the earth—water raining from the sky and various items of your livelihood and sustenance growing from the earth—can you say that there is any creator other than God who bestows these things upon you?

'There is no deity except Him': Without waiting for any reply from their side, He answered it Himself—that there is no deity worthy of worship besides Allah. One reason for answering it Himself is that there can be no correct answer to this question other than this. The second is that the Arab polytheists, who are addressed here, also gave this very answer to this question, as is clear from other places in the Qur'an. It states: when such is the actual reality, and you too do not deny it, then where has your intellect been turned upside down that you have set up other things as deities!

Ayah 4

This is said for the consolation of the Prophet (pbuh)—that if such stubborn people are denying you, it is not a matter of wonder or grief; the messengers who came before you were also denied by similar people. The meaning is that your shortcoming has no share in this; rather, it is entirely due to the specific mindset of these deniers. Leave them to their state. They are following the way of their predecessor nations, so the end of that way which came before them will also come before these people.

'and to Allah are returned [all] matters': All matters are returned to God and will be returned to Him. That is, God is neither neutral nor unconcerned in this matter; rather, everything is and will be brought before Him, and the final decision will be His. So, relying upon Him, continue with your work and leave their matter to Allah. Within this is also an allusion that those who have made others besides Allah their protectors and places of return will one day see the end of their false hopes.

Ayah 5

After consoling the Prophet (pbuh), the opponents are threatened: 'Do not consider what you are being warned of regarding the end of previous nations as an empty threat. This warning of Allah is bound to happen. Let not the comfort of the worldly life that you enjoy today keep you in deception. You assume your present comfort to be a proof of the correctness of your attitude and brush off the warnings of the Prophet (pbuh)—wondering how and from where the punishment could come upon you. But when the time for the manifestation of Allah's promise arrives, you will see that the very thing you were being warned of is standing right before you.'

'and let not the Deceiver deceive you about Allah': 'Gharoora' means a deceiver, and here it refers to Satan, because the greatest deceiver is Satan. It states: 'Let not the deceiver Satan keep you in deception regarding God.' God is highly Merciful and Generous, and He is also highly Retributive and Severe in punishment. Despite all your rebellion and corruption, the respite He has granted you should not lead you to the misconception that you are outside His grasp, or that your assumed partners have saved you, or that they will save you from His grasp. Allah has no partner, nor can anyone save anyone from His grasp. Satan has entangled you in this self-deception only to carry you straight into Hell. It should be kept in mind here that deviation regarding the attributes of God is, in fact, the root of all deviations; therefore, Satan ambushes man from this very point. We are warned of this danger in the verse: 'What has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous?' (Al-Infitar: 6).

Ayah 6

That is, Satan is your enemy, an open enemy, so consider your enemy as an enemy and stay safe from his plots. What kind of wisdom is it that he is your enemy, yet you have made him your guide and handed over your reins to him! If you have handed over your reins to him, then hear his end: the sole purpose of all his activities is to carry those who join his party straight into the depths of the Blaze.

Ayah 7

This is an explanation of that inevitable promise of Allah which was mentioned in verse 5 above. The meaning is that regarding God, let no one remain in the misconception that this world will continue as it is without any distinction between good and bad, or virtue and evil. Those who have assumed this have been deeply deceived by Satan regarding the attributes of God. A Day is necessarily about to come when those who disbelieved will face a severe punishment, and those who led a life of faith and righteous deeds will be deserving of forgiveness and a great reward.

Ayah 8

This is said for the consolation of the Prophet (pbuh)—that if these people are not believing, your shortcoming has no share in this; rather, these are people who have fallen under the retributive law of Allah, so do not exhaust yourself with grief over them. Leave them to their state. Allah is fully aware of what they are doing, and since He is aware, He will deal with them as they deserve.

After 'Then is he for whom the evil of his deed has been made attractive so he considers it good...', the apodosis (jawab) is omitted due to contextual clarity. If this omission is spelled out, the complete statement would be: 'Can you guide the one in whose eyes his evil deed has been made attractive and who has started considering his bad deed as good?' The meaning is that for such people to receive guidance is contrary to the divine law; therefore, there is no need to consume yourself with worry over them. Their matter should be left to Allah. This same point is expressed in verse 19 of Surah Az-Zumar as follows:

'Then, is one who has deserved the decree of punishment [to be guided]? Are you, [O Muhammad], to save one who is in the Fire?'

From this very clause, the reality is clarified that one state of committing an evil is when a person commits it but the feeling of its being an evil remains alive within them. The second state is when their evil is made attractive in their eyes in such a way that they consider it as an art, a fashion, a ladder of progress, and a requirement of civilization and education. In the first state, there remains a hope that if they are warned and reminded, they will regain their senses. But the second state is a sign of the distortion of the heart and the paralysis of the intellect, which the Qur'an has described as the sealing of hearts. The one who is afflicted with this disease has fallen under the retributive law of God; no one can guide them.

'Indeed, Allah sends astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills.' This points to the divine law which Allah Almighty has appointed regarding guidance and misguidance, and which we have been clarifying in various places. Further explanation of this is coming below in this very Surah.

'So do not let yourself go in grief over them. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of what they do.' The meaning is: there is no need to exhaust yourself with grief over such people. They have committed moral and spiritual suicide. If they are not believing, the cause is not that there is any deficiency in your invitation, but rather that their hearts have been sealed. So instead of grieving for them, refer their matter to Allah. He is watching all their deeds and will deal with them as they deserve.

'Hasarat' (grief) can be in the accusative of state (hal) or the accusative of cause (maf'ul lahu); precedents of both are found in Arabic classical texts. In my view, 'alayhim' (over them) relates to 'hasarat', just as in 'ya hasratan 'alal-'ibad'. Its plural form expresses the intensity of grief.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 9–18

Next, as a preface to that inevitable promise mentioned in verse 5 above, its proof is described first. Then it is stated that whoever desires proximity to God and vindication in His court must adopt the correct method, instead of making false deities a medium for proximity. The correct method is that a person walks upon the path of faith and righteous deeds. Faith is that which ascends toward God, and righteous deeds support it and elevate it. Other than this, there is no other path to achieve proximity to God. Those who have abandoned this path and devised other ways, and are active day and night in various conspiracies to turn people away from the actual path, the entire web of their conspiracies will be unraveled one day.

Then it is clarified that increase in wealth and children, decrease or increase in lifespan, and all such matters are in the control of Allah alone; the angels, the Jinn, or other assumed deities have no share in them. The harmony among the opposites in this universe is a highly clear proof that only a single will of the All-Powerful and Self-Sustaining God is operating within it. Besides Him, no one possesses even an atom's weight of authority.

After this, people are warned that this invitation being presented to you is for your own benefit. God has no need of you; rather, you are in need of God. If you accept this invitation, you will reform your world and your Hereafter, and if you do not, you will only destroy yourselves. At the same time, the Prophet (pbuh) is consoled regarding what kind of temperaments will accept this invitation and what kind will remain deprived of it. Recite the verses.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 9

This presents proof from the observations of the universe for that inevitable promise recalled in verse 5 above, showing that you should not consider it improbable. Raising people alive from their graves after death is not in the least difficult for Allah. You constantly observe in this world that the earth lies entirely dry, with no sign of greenery or growth anywhere, until Allah sends the winds from some direction. They stir up and gather the clouds before your eyes. Then We drive them toward a dead land and irrigate it, with which life appears anew in its every corner. It states: 'thus is the Resurrection'—in this very manner will the raising of people anew occur on the Day of Judgment. The meaning is: since Allah is constantly giving the nation an observation of dying and being raised alive after death in this world, why do you assume the Resurrection to be impossible!... We have been clarifying in various places in this book that this world, in its very existence, is an excellent place of learning for all those realities which the Qur'an teaches. If man utilizes intellect and insight, he can find proof for every claim of the Qur'an to his right and left.

In this verse, the style of discourse is also noteworthy. First, the past tense 'arsala' (sent) is used. Then comes the continuous/present tense of 'futheeru' (stir up). After that, the first-person plural past tense verbs 'suqnahu' (We drove it) and 'ahyayna' (We gave life) appear. This diversity of style possesses various beauties, the details of which are not the subject here. Just remember that the past tense comes merely to state the event, the continuous tense contains the aspect of portraying the scene, and the first-person pronoun indicates special attention and favor.

Ayah 10

The meaning is that the Resurrection is inevitable and everyone has to be presented before Allah. As for the question: who will achieve honor and vindication before God and who will face disgrace and failure? Those who desire the correct answer to this question must remember that honor belongs entirely to Allah Almighty; therefore, whoever achieves it will achieve it only through connection with Him and His bestowing of it.

'To Him ascends good speech, and righteous work raises it.' This describes the method of achieving this honor—that the thing which ascends from the servants toward Allah is 'good speech' (al-kalim al-tayyib), i.e., the declaration of faith. Other than this, there is no other thing which can serve as a means of mediation and proximity to God. The thing which supports this declaration of faith and elevates it is righteous work. Without righteous work, the declaration of faith remains withered. It is as if the declaration of faith is like a grapevine which in itself is highly fruitful, but its freshness and fruitfulness depend entirely upon receiving a support upon which it can climb, spread, and blossom. This support is achieved through righteous work. Righteous work nurtures it and makes it fruitful. Otherwise, just as a grapevine remains shriveled without support, faith remains withered without righteous work.

Hazrat Ibn Abbas (ra) interpreted 'al-kalim al-tayyib' as the declaration of faith, and the mention of righteous work side by side with it here is itself a testimony to this. The word 'tayyib' (good/fertile) points to the fertility and fruitfulness of this declaration, because from the perspective of the philosophy of religion, this declaration is the root of all knowledge and wisdom. Whoever attains it has attained the key to the treasure of all knowledge and wisdom, and this reality is also accepted—that faith and righteous work are mutually dependent. Just as without faith work has no foundation, without work faith is a lifeless thing. To fully understand this reality, have a look at what we wrote under verses 24–26 of Surah Ibrahim.

This is said here to clarify to the polytheists: 'You have assumed your angel deities to be a medium for achieving honor and elevation before God, whereas honor belongs only to Allah, and the sole means of achieving proximity to Allah is that faith which is supported by righteous work.' In the Qur'an, this is repeatedly quoted regarding the polytheists—that they considered their deities a source of honor and vindication before God. For instance:

'We only worship them that they may bring us nearer to Allah in position.' (Al-Zumar: 3)

In another place:

'And they have taken besides Allah gods that they might be for them an honor.' (Maryam: 81)

This strikes at this very illusion of theirs—implying: emerge from this fool's paradise, and if you seek honor before God, adopt the path of faith and righteous deeds.

'But they who plot evil deeds will have a severe punishment': That is, those who, instead of accepting this reality, are carrying out various evil plots to protect their pride and to harm the religion of Allah and His messenger, should remember that they will not achieve honor in this way, but are rather preparing a severe punishment for themselves.

In this clause, the accusative case (nasb) of 'sayyi'at' (evil deeds) feels somewhat unusual, as the verb 'makara' (plot) is not typically transitive in this manner. The author of 'Al-Kashshaf' explained it by stating that 'sayyi'at' here is an adjective qualifying an omitted cognate accusative (masdar)—meaning: 'yamburuna al-makarati al-sayyi'at' (they plot evil plottings). This explanation appears correct. Its precedent is present in verse 43 below.

'and the plotting of those - it will perish': That is, those who are active day and night in conspiracies to keep themselves and others in this self-deception should remember that one day all their conspiracies and activities will perish. Here, the repetition of the subject ('ulma'ika') adds great force to the discourse—indicating that these conspiracies of theirs will not harm anyone else, but the destruction will fall upon their own conspiracies and will become the cause of their own ruin. Below, this theme is opened up: 'but the evil plot does not encompass except its authors' (43)—because the truth is a shared asset and a source of welfare for everyone. If someone opposes it, they only poke out their own eyes and strike their own foot with an axe.

Ayah 11

That is, whatever increase or decrease occurs in children and lifespan occurs by the knowledge of Allah and His command. Even in these things, your assumed deities have no share. For this reason, you must turn to God alone for these things as well. You should not seek the support of anyone else besides Him. It should be kept in mind here that wealth, children, health, and life, and other such desires have always been among the factors of polytheism. Those who do not have children, or have them but they do not survive, or have only daughters and no sons—such people, if superstitious, are easily turned by Satan toward shrines, tombs, and graves. This factor also played a major role in the polytheism of the Arab polytheists. They did not consider their deities as creators of the heavens and the earth, but they considered them—especially the angels—highly influential in matters of sustenance, wealth, and children. Similarly, in matters of sickness and death, they worshipped the Jinn, considering them to possess control. We have mentioned in the commentary of Surah Al-An'am that they considered some Jinn so dangerous that they sacrificed their own children for them. Their idea was that if they did not keep them pleased by sacrificing a son or daughter, they would consume all their children. Similarly, those afflicted with some disease also turned to these deities and prayed to them for health and long life. This verse strikes at these very superstitions.

'And Allah created you from dust, then from a sperm-drop, then He made you mates.' This clause serves as a preface for the subsequent theme. It draws attention to the accepted reality that it is Allah Who created Adam from dust, then ran his progeny from a sperm-drop, and then made you into pairs. The meaning is: since the entire house was built and populated by God, and this is a reality which even you do not deny, then what difficulty arose for God after populating this house that He was forced to entrust the matters of its sustenance, children, and lifespan to others? Can He Who can create you from dust, run your progeny from a drop of water, and settle you as husband and wife, be incapable of managing your sustenance, your children, and your lifespan, such that He would make you dependent upon other thresholds for them!

'And no female conceives nor does she give birth except with His knowledge.' This points to the logical consequence of the above preface—that He Who settled this family of Adam knows very well when a female conceived, whether there is a male or female in her womb, when she will give birth to it, and in what form she will give birth to it! Reference to knowledge is specifically made here because the Arab polytheists did not deny God's being the Creator; their doubt was: how can God have knowledge of every single matter of such a vast world? Because of this, they believed He had entrusted many matters relating to sustenance, lifespan, and children to His partners. The Qur'an strikes at this very illusion with the words: 'except with His knowledge'—indicating that not even the smallest event occurs in this world that is outside the sphere of its Creator's knowledge.

'And no aged person is granted [additional] life nor is there lessened from his lifespan except that it is in a register.' The word 'mu'ammar' (aged person) here is not used specifically in the sense of a long-lived person, but refers to anyone who is granted any lifespan, whether long or short; the author of 'Al-Kashshaf' took this very meaning, and in our view, it is correct.

This is a further explanation of the above theme—that whatever lifespan is granted to anyone, and whatever hours and days are decreased from that lifespan, occur by the command and knowledge of God. 'except that it is in a register' stands in place of the above words 'except with His knowledge', making the above theme even more robust and certified. That is, not only does God know all these things, but every single thing is recorded in a great book. Do not remain in the illusion that God will forget anything.

'Indeed, that for Allah is easy.' This removes the doubt to which we have pointed above—that the heart of the Arabs was in the illusion: how can God alone encompass all particulars of affairs? Under this illusion, they invented partners and helpers to assist God and then became so engrossed in their worship that God became a marginalized entity for them. This refutes their illusion—stating that none of these tasks is difficult for God such that He would stand in need of any partner or helper.

Ayah 12-13

This is a refutation of polytheism and partners from another aspect. The conflict of opposites in this universe is also among the highly important factors of polytheism. The Qur'an has refuted it from various aspects, and we have been clarifying it. Here too, this is pointed out—that the existence of opposites in the world is not a proof that different wills are operating within it, because alongside their apparent opposition, a highly deep harmony is also found in these opposites, which testifies that a supreme, all-encompassing, and multi-dimensional dominant power is controlling all these opposites, using them under its wisdom for the collective welfare of this universe.

'And not equal are the two seas...': That is: see, there are two seas, and both are entirely different from one another in their apparent characteristics and attributes. The water of one is sweet, thirst-quenching, and pleasant, while the other is salty and bitter. These two collide with one another, but it does not happen that the salty sea dominates the sweet sea to make it salty, or that the sweet sea changes the nature of the salty one; rather, a supreme power, despite their collision, has kept them bound to their limits. This same reality is pointed out in Surah Ar-Rahman: 'He released the two seas, meeting [side by side]; Between them is a barrier [so] neither of them transgresses' (19-20).

Then see how, despite their opposition and difference, they perform the service of a higher purpose—that from both of them, in your long sea journeys where obtaining fresh meat is otherwise impossible, you obtain fresh meat, and along with food, you also extract precious pearls from them for your adornment. Then you see that they are subjected to you in such a way that your ships sail upon their chest, cleaving through the waves, so that by traveling from one place to another you may seek Allah's provision and favor and remain grateful to Him. The meaning is: do not remain lost in the apparent opposition of this universe, but look also at the mutual harmony of these opposites, and the reality will become bright: that a single All-Powerful and Self-Sustaining God has brought this world into existence, and He, by His power and wisdom, has put all its varying components in the service of man so that man remains grateful to his Lord.

'He causes the night to enter the day...': Attention is drawn to this same reality through another example—that the same is the case with the night that comes every day and the day that appears every day. They also apparently bear the relation of opposites, but a complete harmony of pairs is found between them. Both are essential for the survival of this world and for your rest and livelihood. The same is the case with the sun and the moon. Foolish people made them deities and worshipped them, whereas they testify by their very existence that God has subjected them to the service of His creation. Thus, both remain continuously active in your service with their specified system of times. 'That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs sovereignty.' This is the summary of the entire above discussion—that this same Allah, Whose glory, powers, and wisdoms you observe, is your Lord, and in His power lies the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth.

'And those whom you invoke besides Him do not possess [as much as] the membrane of a date stone.' 'Qitmeer' refers to that thin membrane that is over the seed of a date.

This is the same point stated in a negative style—meaning: as for those whom you call upon and worship besides Allah, they do not possess even a shred of authority in the creation and management of this world.

Ayah 14

'Istajaba lahu' means he answered him or he came to his rescue.

This clarifies the helplessness and insignificance of their assumed deities—stating that if you call upon them for help in any of your difficulties, first of all, they will not hear your call, and even if they were to hear, they would not come to your rescue. This helplessness of theirs is obvious in this world and will become even more so in the Hereafter. First of all, most of the things the polytheists worshipped had no actual existence; therefore, the question of their hearing or coming to the rescue does not even arise. And if they worshipped some entities that do exist, then first, in the Hereafter they will declare their ignorance—stating: 'We have no knowledge that they used to worship us.' Second, those among them who are righteous—such as the angels and the prophets—will openly declare their disassociation. And those who are wicked—such as the Jinn and the devils—will reply to those who called upon them: 'It was your own misfortune that you worshipped us; now face the consequences of it. Today neither can you be of any use to us, nor can we come to your rescue.'

'And on the Day of Resurrection they will deny your association.' That is: in this world, they are unaware of your call and helpless to rescue you, and on the Day of Resurrection, they will deny your polytheism. Thus, the detail of the angels' denial has passed in Surah Saba in these words:

'And [remember] the Day He will gather them all and then say to the angels, "Did these people use to worship you?" They will say, "Exalted are You! You are our benefactor, not them. Rather, they used to worship the jinn; most of them were believers in them."' (Saba: 40–41)

'And none can inform you like one who is All-Aware.' This is a warning at the end, and a highly powerful and eloquent warning. The indefiniteness (tankeer) of 'Khabeer' (All-Aware) is to magnify its status. The meaning is: listen to these words and listen to them well, because what lies behind the veils of the unseen and what is about to appear before you tomorrow is known only to a true Knower of reality. No one can inform you of these realities better than Him. It will be your misfortune if you do not value this and remain entangled in false hopes!

Ayah 15-17

This is an addition to the same warning, and a highly powerful addition. The address is with the words 'O mankind,' so that everyone opens their ears to hear: this entire arrangement of teaching and reminding, and the fact that God's messenger has combined his day and night to wake and shake people, is not because any work of God is stuck due to people's lack of faith. God is entirely self-sufficient (Al-Ghani) and praised in His essence (Al-Hameed). However, you yourselves are in need of God, in this world as well as in the Hereafter. The self-sufficiency of God is such that if He wills, He can destroy you all and bring another new creation to settle. If He wishes to do so, this task is not in the least difficult for Him. It is merely His mercy and favor upon you that despite your disregard and ungratefulness, He continues to grant you respite. It is better that you benefit from this respite; otherwise, remember that you will harm neither God nor His messenger, but only yourselves.

We have explained 'He is the Self-Sufficient, the Praiseworthy' in another place—stating that God is perfect in His essence. This perfection of His does not stand in need of anything outside of His essence. At the same time, He is 'Al-Hameed'—meaning characterized by all attributes of praise. His relation with creation is not based on any need, but entirely upon His mercy and favor:

'I did not create the creation to gain any benefit,
But I created the creation to show My generosity.'

Ayah 18

'And if a heavily laden soul calls [another] to [carry some of] its load, nothing of it will be carried, even if he should be a close relative.' Before 'muthqalatan' (heavily laden), the noun qualified (mawsoof) is omitted—meaning: 'nafsun muthqalatan' (a soul heavily laden with its load of sin). Similarly, before 'himliha' (its load), a prefixed noun (mudaf) is omitted, and after 'kana' (should be), its noun 'al-mad'u' (the one called upon) is omitted. All these omissions are in accordance with the well-known rules of high Arabic.

The meaning is that if a soul laden with its sins calls upon anyone to support it in carrying its burden, no one will share its burden, even if the one called upon for help is a close relative. The righteous will not share it because when they were not its partners in sins in the world, why should they be its partners in the Hereafter? And the wicked will be crushed under their own burdens, so how could they support anyone else!

'You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen and have established prayer.' This is said for the consolation of the Prophet (pbuh)—that you inform people of these realities. If they are warned, well and good. If they are not, leave them to their state. Your warning can be effective only upon those who utilize their intellect and capabilities of hearing, sight, and heart, fear their Lord while He is unseen, and establish prayer. As for those who want to believe only after seeing everything with their eyes, you have no cure for them. Leave their matter to Us.

'and have established prayer' is mentioned here as the primary fruit of faith and as a sign of fearing the Lord unseen. Whoever fears God unseen necessarily establishes prayer. The one who is indifferent to prayer is indifferent to God, even if they make high-sounding claims of faith verbally.

'And whoever purifies himself only purifies himself for [the benefit of] his soul. And to Allah is the destination.' This points to the benefit of prayer—that whoever wishes to be relieved of the burden of sins and become purified should establish prayer instead of relying on false supports. This will purify them of sins, and whoever attains purification does good only to themselves, because Allah is not in need of anyone's worship and obedience; rather, the servants themselves are in need of Him, and the return of all must ultimately be to Him.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 19–28

Next, the reality is clarified: what kind of people will believe and what kind will remain deprived of this blessing. To explain this reality, attention is drawn to the existence of opposites in this universe—stating: you see that within it there is light and also darkness, heat and also cold. Similarly, in the earth, there are fertile plots that flourish as soon as a drop of rain falls, and barren regions that remain desolate even after a thousand rains. In the same way, among individuals, there are those possessing different kinds of capabilities. Those whose capabilities are alive will benefit from this rain of mercy which has descended in the form of the Qur'an, and those whose capabilities are dead will remain deprived of its benefit. Just as Allah Almighty created light and darkness, and cold and heat, for the collective welfare of this universe, He has also given respite to those who prefer falsehood alongside those who seek the truth. This is because within this conflict of truth and falsehood, the metal of the people of truth is refined, and proof of Allah is established against the people of falsehood. Recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 19

'Blind' here refers to those who are blind of intellect and heart, and 'seeing' refers to those whose capabilities of intellect and heart are alive and who utilize them. This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh)—that in the matter of accepting your invitation, the state of everyone will not be the same. Those who have blinded the eyes of their intellect and whose hearts have become devoid of light are not going to accept your invitation, no matter how much you try. Only those who possess insight will benefit from your light. The law of Allah Almighty is that those who keep alive the natural capabilities bestowed by Him and value them, He increases them in insight and guidance; and those who do not value these capabilities, let alone being granted more, even those that were granted to them are stripped away. This same theme is expressed in Surah An-Naml in these words:

'Indeed, you cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf hear the call when they turn their backs, fleeing. And you cannot guide the blind away from their error. You will only make hear those who believe in Our verses.' (An-Naml: 80–81)

Ayah 20-21

It states that just as in this world there is darkness and light, and shade and heat, and these two cannot be equal, similarly, these two kinds of people mentioned above cannot be equal. However, just as the welfare of this world is inherent in both darkness and light, and shade and heat, similarly, the welfare of nature is inherent in the existence of these kinds of people. The mention of this welfare has occurred in other places in the Qur'an. It is clarified there that since Allah Almighty created this world to test human choice, the requirement of His wisdom was that those who do not value the capabilities bestowed by Him and spend their lives as worshippers of falsehood instead of truth should also receive respite to a specified extent.

In this, the repetition of the second 'la' (not) has been considered redundant by some grammarians. But in our view, it is not redundant, but for emphasis. Precedents of this have passed before.

Ayah 22

This is the same theme expressed in other words and another style. During this period, the Prophet (pbuh) was deeply troubled by the indifference of his nation. He feared that some shortcoming on his part might be involved in it. To remove this worry of his, Allah Almighty consoled him in various styles—stating: 'Your job is to make the living hear; waking up the dead is not your responsibility.' Those whose capabilities are alive are listening to and accepting your word. As for those who have become completely dead intellectually and spiritually, if you want them to become listeners of your word, this is not going to happen.

'Indeed, Allah makes hear whom He wills.' 'Isma' (making hear) is used here in the sense of granting the capability to hear and accept the truth. That is, it is Allah Who grants the capability to hear and accept the truth to whomever He pleases. Every willing of Allah Almighty is under His wisdom. His law in this matter is that those who keep alive the natural capabilities bestowed by Him, He grants them further capability to hear, understand, and accept; and those who do not value their natural guidance are like the dead in a moral sense, and reviving them is beyond the power of anyone but God.

This clearly defines the limits of the Prophet's (pbuh) responsibility in the matter of propagation and invitation—stating that you are only a warner to warn people of the coming danger. It is not your responsibility that people must accept your word. God has sent you with the truth. Every matter of which you are giving news is bound to happen. So your job is that those who lend their ears to your words, give them the glad tidings of their blessed end; and those who turn away from your words, warn them of their bad end. You have no responsibility beyond this. For this reason, you need not be overly troubled for them.

'And there was no nation but that there had passed within it a warner.' That is, you are not the first warner in the world; rather, in the nations that passed in the past, Allah also sent His warners. In their stories, there is a lesson for you and a warning for your nation. Allah will deal with you in the same way He dealt with His messengers, and He will necessarily deal with your nation in the same way He dealt with previous nations.

Ayah 25-26

This is an explanation of the same summary. It states that if these people are denying you, it is nothing new. The nations that passed before them similarly denied their respective messengers. This tradition has been ongoing from the past. Do not think that your nation is the first to deal with you in this manner. The meaning is: if this situation had appeared for the first time, then indeed it would have been a cause of concern for you—fearing that some shortcoming on your part might be involved; but when every messenger was treated by his nation in this very way, it shows that the approach of nations toward their messengers has always been the same.

'Their messengers came to them with clear proofs...': That is, it was also not the case that their messengers came empty-handed. Rather, they came with highly clear proofs, miracles, scriptures, and the enlightening Book, but none of these things could convince them. The meaning is: do not expect even from your opponents that if some sign is shown to them according to their demand, they will believe. For this kind of people, all signs, all scriptures, and all books are useless.

'Bayyinat' (clear proofs) and 'Zubur' (scriptures) are general. Allah Almighty sent every messenger armed with them. 'Al-Kitab al-Muneer' (the enlightening Book) refers to the Torah. Before the Qur'an, it held the status of the enlightening Book.

'Then I seized those who disbelieved': The way the nations adopted was mentioned above. This describes the way Allah Almighty dealt with them. It states that when they denied the messenger, We seized them, and when We seized them, see how We seized them and what an exemplary punishment We gave them! The meaning is: if your nation also remains firm on its current way, We will deal with them in the same manner. Our law is the same for everyone.

Ayah 27

The same reality that was explained in verses 20–22 is now explained in the light of other examples of this universe. It is as if verses 23–26 came in the middle as an aside. After their conclusion, the discourse is reconnected with its actual sequence. It states: do you not see that when Allah sends down rain from the sky, not a single kind of thing grows from it, but various kinds and various colors of things grow. From the same rain, beautiful, colorful, and tasty fruits grow, and from the same rain, wild bushes grow in low-quality soils. The same is the case with this Qur'anic rain. From it, the capabilities of righteous natures will flourish, and those who harbor malice and corruption will also come to the forefront. This is because Satan will not tolerate this peacefully, but will also incite his progeny to oppose it. The word 'alwan' (colors) in Arabic is used not only in the sense of literal colors, but also in the sense of kinds and varieties. Further below in this very verse, this word is used in this sense.

In Surah Al-A'raf, attention is drawn to this aspect of the effects of rain in these words:

'And the good land - its vegetation emerges by permission of its Lord; but that which is bad - its vegetation does not emerge except with difficulty.' (Al-A'raf: 58)

To understand this explanation, have a look at what we wrote under that verse.

'And among the mountains are tracts, white and red of varying colors and [others] extremely black.' 'Judad' is the plural of 'juddah'. This word primarily refers to those stripes of different colors found on the backs of wild asses and mules, but here it is used for those rocks and strata of varying colors whose stripes or rows are found within mountains.

'Gharabeeb' is the plural of 'gharbeeb', which means pitch-black. This word usually comes to emphasize 'aswad' (black). For example, they say: 'aswad gharbeeb'—meaning such and such thing is pitch-black. For this reason, the question arises: why did it come before 'sud' (black) here, as placing the emphasis first is contrary to eloquence? Grammarians have given various answers to this question. Some have taken it in the sense of an appositive (badal) that comes for clarification. If this explanation is accepted, it is correct from the aspect that the appositive which comes for clarification is indeed for emphasis. In this case, the speaker, as it were, repeats his statement in simple words before the addressee so that it is well impressed upon their mind. The reason for drawing attention specifically to the black stones with this clarification is that the objective is to explain: just as there are black stones in the mountains, there are black-hearted individuals among people; and just as white, red, and black stones have different uses, black-hearted individuals also have a place and use for which nature has granted them respite in this world.

Ayah 28

It states that like inanimate things, this diversity and variety is also prominent among humans, beasts, and cattle. The word 'alwan' (colors) here too is not used only in the sense of literal colors, but in the wide sense of kinds and varieties. That is, a great difference and variation is found among them in terms of appearance, character, attributes, temperament, features, and habits. The meaning is: just as milk cannot be expected from every animal, the fear of God cannot be expected from every person. Only those among Allah's servants will fear Him within whose hearts lies the light of knowledge and gnosis.

The word 'Ulama' (those who have knowledge) here is not used in its technical, modern sense, but in its true linguistic meaning. Just as in verse 18 above, it was stated: 'You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen,' similarly here, it is stated: 'Only those fear Allah, from among His servants, who have knowledge.' This shows that one type of people are those who are mere literalists; they see nothing beyond physical sensations, nor do they try to see beyond them. The second type are those who possess the capability to reach from the apparent to the hidden, and from the metaphor to the reality, and who give more importance to the demands of their intellect and spirit than to the physical demands of their stomach. In reality, these are the people who are the cream of humanity, and who are truly deserving of the title of 'Ulama', and they are the ones who fear Allah, and the invitation of Allah's messengers appeals to them.

'Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Forgiving.' Just as the preceding statement was made to console the Prophet (pbuh), the reference to the attributes 'Al-Aziz' (Exalted in Might) and 'Al-Ghafoor' (Forgiving) is also for your consolation. The meaning is: those who are rejecting your invitation today, God can seize them whenever He wills, but alongside being 'Al-Aziz', He is also 'Al-Ghafoor'. For this reason, as long as His wisdom demands, He grants respite to such people.

From this verse, it is learned that the source of true knowledge is, in reality, the gnosis of Allah. Whoever has not attained the gnosis of God is entirely deprived of knowledge, even if they memorize the books of the entire world. Similarly, it is also learned that whoever possesses the gnosis of God will necessarily possess the fear of God within them. If a person is devoid of the fear of God, it means they are also devoid of His gnosis. This gnosis and fear produce true life in all the sciences and thoughts of man, due to which sciences and arts become a source of goodness and blessing for the world. If this thing is absent, then all of man's intelligence is spent in serving Satan's purposes, and it ultimately becomes a cause of destruction.

Here, a doubt might arise in someone's mind: from the aforementioned verses, it emerges that just as Allah Almighty has created various kinds of things in this workshop of the universe, and in the creation of all of them lies His wisdom and purpose, similarly, the existence of those who oppose the truth is also not devoid of God's will and wisdom. When the situation is such, on what basis do these people deserve blame and punishment? Although we have been clearing this doubt in the lines above, the correct attitude is that a person should align with the urges of their nature; but since they are not compelled like animals and inanimate things, but possess free will, many of them violate their nature and the commands of their Lord. For this misuse of their free will, they deserve punishment. But despite this, Allah Almighty grants them respite so that the proof of Allah is fully established against them, and their opposition of the truth serves as a test for the people of truth. If you reflect on this aspect, the reality becomes clear: from one perspective, their existence is in accordance with wisdom, and nature grants them respite in this world for a specified time for this very wisdom; but from another perspective, they deserve punishment because they did not value their capabilities of hearing and sight, and misused their choice in following the desires of the self.

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 29–38

The point described in principle in the preceding passage is now applied to facts and situations in the subsequent verses. That is, it clarifies: who among the inheritors of previous nations are truly deserving of the title of 'Ulama' and will believe in this Qur'an, and what attitude the righteous or wicked of the nation whom Allah has now chosen for inheriting His Book will adopt toward it. Then, the results of the deeds of both are clarified. Recite the verses.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 29

Those who were called 'Ulama' in verse 28 above, and in whose praise it was stated: 'Only those fear Allah, from among His servants, who have knowledge,' are described in detail here. It states that those who recite the Book of Allah, establish prayer, and spend out of the provision and favor bestowed by God generously, secretly and openly in His way, are the ones who fear Allah. And this spending of theirs is a transaction for which there is no fear of any decline in the future, but rather its blessings will continuously increase.

Although this allusion can refer to all those righteous figures who were characterized by these attributes, my mind goes toward the fact that this points to those scholars of the People of the Book who believed in the Qur'an, and whom the Qur'an has praised in various places. In my view, the context for this is that after their mention, the mention of the righteous and wicked of the Children of Ismail is coming independently in verse 32, and their mention is with the words: 'Then We caused to inherit the Book those We have chosen of Our servants.' Here, the word 'thumma' (then) is a proof that the people mentioned above refer to those who were made inheritors of the Book prior to the Qur'an, and they remained firm on it by the grace of Allah, due to the blessing of which they were granted the honor of believing in the Qur'an after its revelation.

Here, in the praise of these righteous among the People of the Book, three things are mentioned specifically: the recitation of the Book of Allah, the establishment of prayer, and spending in the way of Allah. The reason for this is that these three things are the true protectors of religion and the actual signs of the fear of God, and it is described with great clarity in the Qur'an that the Jews had forgotten their Book, lost their prayer, and were overcome by the disease of miserliness and greed for gold; because of this, they remained deprived of the honor of believing in the Qur'an. Only those among them believed in the Qur'an who remained safe from these diseases.

'expect a transaction that will never perish' points to the reality that those who made this worldly life their goal, filled their safes with forbidden and permissible earnings, and tried to become Rockefellers, their fate one day will be that all their wealth will fly away as dust and ashes. However, those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah are investing their capital in a business in which there is never any fear of loss.

Ayah 30

This points to the motivation behind their spending, and is also a promise of a full reward from Allah Almighty—indicating that whatever they spend, they spend neither for show nor display, nor do they desire any compensation for it, nor do they boast of any favor, but spend only for the pleasure of Allah Almighty so that He may give them its full reward and increase it further out of His favor. Thus, their Lord will deal with them according to their expectations, because He is Forgiving of shortcomings and appreciative of His servants' deeds. Here, the reference to the attributes 'Ghafoor' (Forgiving) and 'Shakoor' (Appreciative) is to encourage the servants—showing that in the matter of accepting their virtues, Allah Almighty will show great overlooking, and will accept even their small deeds with appreciation and honor.

Ayah 31

This is an invitation of faith to the righteous among the People of the Book—stating that the Book We have revealed to you is indeed the truth. Therefore, all seekers of truth should accept it immediately and cherish it. The restrictiveness in 'He is the Truth' (Huwal-Haqq) points to the reality that the scripture Allah gave to the People of the Book before this was turned by them, through their distortions, into a collection of arbitrary things, because of which they remained deprived of the blessing of the truth. Now, Allah has highlighted it anew in an entirely unmixed and perfect form in the shape of this Book, so that it serves as a source of guidance for creation.

'confirming what was before it': This is that proof of the truth of this Book which has occurred in various places in the Qur'an specifically to establish proof against the People of the Book—showing that this Book is the fulfillment of those prophecies that were already present in previous scriptures regarding it. We have already given references to these prophecies in detail in their proper places.

'Indeed, Allah, of His servants, is All-Aware and Seeing.' It states that Allah made this arrangement for protecting this truth because He is aware of the state of His servants and is continuously watching over their reform and corruption. Thus, when He saw that they were deprived, through the treachery of the guardians, of the guidance with which He had blessed them, He arranged for its protection anew. If He had not made this arrangement, it would have meant that He is entirely unconcerned with the good and evil of His creation. However, He is not unconcerned, but is aware of everything and keeps watch over every particular.

Ayah 32

This refers to the arrangement which Allah Almighty made to benefit His creation with His guidance. 'Those We chose of Our servants' refers here to the Children of Ismail, or in other words, the 'Unlettered Arabs' as a community, among whom Allah Almighty had promised Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) to send His final messenger through whom creation was to receive the final and perfect Shariah. It states: We chose another group of Our servants and made them inheritors of Our Book. The words 'made them inheritors' point to the reality that the group who was previously made inheritors of the Book of Allah (meaning the Children of Israel) was stripped of this trust, and it was bestowed upon another group chosen for this honor, i.e., the Children of Ismail.

This same honor of the unlettered is pointed out in the verse of Surah Al-Jumu'ah: 'He it is Who sent among the unlettered a messenger from themselves...' (2), and the Qur'an has repeatedly referred to this honor of theirs to invite them to faith—so that they may value it. If they do not value it, they will deprive themselves of the honor of the leadership of the world for which Allah chose them. This honor of the unlettered is mentioned in Surah An-Nisa' as follows:

'Or do they envy people for what Allah has given them of His bounty? But We had already given the family of Abraham the Scripture and wisdom and conferred upon them a great kingdom. And some of them believed in it, and some of them averted [people] from it. And sufficient is Hell as a blaze.' (An-Nisa': 54–55)

To understand the verse under discussion, have a look at what we wrote under that verse. Just as in that verse, the Children of Ismail are meant as a community and they are described as being given the Scripture, wisdom, and a great kingdom, similarly, the chosen status pointed out in the verse under discussion was received by them as a community. The Jews deeply envied this chosen status of theirs, and a major motivation behind their opposition of the Prophet (pbuh) was that they understood that if his prophethood was established, the religious leadership which they had enjoyed until then would be transferred to their rivals, i.e., the Children of Ismail. The Qur'an has repeatedly informed the Arabs of this malice of the Children of Israel, and invited them to value the honor with which Allah had blessed them and the great office for which He chose them, and not to assist their enemies by opposing it. Similarly, here, reference to this chosen status is made with this very purpose—that these people value this favor and bounty of Allah Almighty and do not remain deprived of it.

'And among them is he who wrongs himself, and among them is he who is moderate, and among them is he who is foremost in good deeds by permission of Allah.' This describes the attitude they adopted as individuals in response to this favor of Allah Almighty. It states that there are three kinds of people among them. First, those who wrong themselves—meaning they are dedicating all their energy to opposing it. Neither are they ready to accept it themselves, nor do they want to let anyone else accept it. In the aforementioned verse of Surah An-Nisa', this group is mentioned with the words: 'and some of them averted [people] from it'—meaning they turned away from it themselves and also tried to prevent others. It should be clear that the word 'sadda' comes in both senses of turning away and preventing. This group is described here with the words 'who wrongs himself.' That is: through this opposition, they are wronging their own souls by preparing for themselves deprivation from God's greatest favor and the punishment of Hell; otherwise, as far as the religion of God is concerned, it is not going to suffer any harm from their opposition. In the Qur'an, 'zulm' (wronging/injustice) is generally used to describe polytheism. In Surah As-Saffat, it is: 'and among their descendants is the doer of good and the clearly unjust to himself' (113). Commentators have generally interpreted 'those We chose of Our servants' to mean the Muslim Ummah, and then—who knows on what basis—they have derived the conclusion from this that even these wrongdoers will be forgiven. We have specified the correct context of the verse, so there remains no need to refute this view. In our support, there are also traditions from Hazrat Ibn Abbas (ra) and Mujahid. If required, have a look in the Tafseer of Ibn Kathir or Tafseer of Ibn Jarir. These great commentators interpreted 'who wrongs himself' as those people who are mentioned in Surah Al-Waqi'ah as the 'companions of the left'.

'and among them is he who is moderate': This is the mention of the second group. 'Muqtacid' means moderate. That is, these people do not oppose it, but they also do not gather the courage to step forward in support of this invitation of truth. Their not opposing it is proof that they possess the feeling of its being true, but this feeling is not strong enough for them to stand up in its support regardless of all consequences. The Qur'an has used this word for them to give hope that these people are not going to remain deprived of this blessing like the first group (who wrongs themselves), but sooner or later their hesitation will be removed and, if Allah wills, they will become active supporters of this invitation.

'and among them is he who is foremost in good deeds': This is the mention of the third group. These are those people within whom the consciousness of truth is so strong that every invitation of truth appeals to them immediately, and their willpower is so robust that when something appeals to them, they cross all obstacles of the path in a single leap, and do not hesitate to make even the greatest sacrifice in its support or defense, but rather face all hurdles with full courage and dedicate their life to excel in every field of goodness and virtue. This points to the foremost-pioneers of Islam, at whose head stood Sayyina Abu Bakr As-Siddiq (ra).

The restriction 'by permission of Allah' with this group's 'foremost in good deeds' shows the reality that this high rank is not everyone's share, but Allah alone bestows this rank upon whomever He pleases. This refers to the divine law which Allah Almighty has appointed regarding progression and delay in His path, and to which we have pointed in more than one place in this book.

'That is what is the great bounty': This describes the high rank which these foremost in good deeds will achieve. And in this lies a highly eloquent invitation for the 'moderate' ones—implying: the opportunity still remains for them to try their fortune to become partners in this great bounty; so they should abandon hesitation and step forward with courage. We limit our discussion to these allusions here. God willing, in the commentary of Surah Al-Waqi'ah, all its aspects will come under discussion.

Ayah 33

This describes the reward of these valiant ones in the Hereafter—stating that for them will be gardens of perpetual residence which they will enter. That is, their entry into these gardens will not be for a mere temporary walk or recreation, but for eternity. 'Yuhallawna' (they will be adorned), the passive voice, is a proof of honor and respect. The mention of gold bracelets, pearls, and silk, etc., is to make the addressee imagine the blessings of Paradise. The true reality of these things is known only to Allah Almighty. Their correct estimate will be formed only when they appear before us in the Hereafter. In ancient times, kings wore gold bracelets and pearls; for this reason, this expression was adopted to give an idea of the garments of Paradise.

Ayah 34-35

That is, when the people of Paradise see the truth of all of Allah's promises with their own eyes, the word of gratitude will flow spontaneously from their tongues: 'Praise be to Allah, all those promises of our Lord for which we lived and died have been fulfilled far beyond our expectations. He has removed all our grief. Now neither does any regret of the past remain, nor any fear of the future. Our Lord is Forgiving and Appreciative. He overlooked our shortcomings and blessed our every single virtue with acceptance.' This same word of gratitude of the people of Paradise is mentioned in another place as follows: 'And the last of their call will be, "Praise to Allah, Lord of the worlds"' (Yunus: 10).

'the home of duration' (dar al-muqamah) refers to the home of eternal residence—meaning: before this we were in a transient inn in the world, and we considered it only as a transient inn. Now our Lord has settled us in such a home of His favor and mercy which is to remain forever. Within it, there is neither any labor or hardship for us, nor any fatigue and depression. It should be clear that even if the best of comforts is achieved, a person becomes depressed within it if freshness and variety are not continuously maintained according to their desire. Allah Almighty has made His Paradise such that within it such unadulterated and diverse comfort will be achieved that the people of Paradise will not feel any boredom within it.

Ayah 36-37

This describes the end of the disbelievers—stating that Allah Almighty will cast them into Hell, where their condition will be such that death will not come to them so that they might get release from this punishment by dying, nor will their punishment be lightened for them so that they might take a breath. After 'min 'adhabiha' (from its punishment), the words 'so that they might rest' or a similar expression is omitted due to contextual clarity.

'Thus do We reward every ungrateful one.' That is: this which We are narrating is not merely the story of others, but We punish every ungrateful and wicked person in this very way. The meaning is that those people will also deserve this punishment who are denying Our Book and Our messenger today. With this sentence, the discourse becomes entirely relevant to the present situation.

'And they will cry out therein...': They will call out while lying in this Hell: 'O our Lord, grant us release from here once so that we may go to the world and do righteous deeds, entirely different from what we used to do.'

'Did We not grant you life enough for whoever would remember therein to remember, and the warner had come to you?': They will receive this reply from Allah Almighty: 'Did you not receive enough respite in which a seeker of warning could take warning? Moreover, an alerting messenger had also come to you! So now for what do you ask respite? Now no one can be your helper; now taste the punishment whose causes you yourself provided.'

From this verse, the indication emerges that those to whom Allah Almighty gave sufficient lifespan and against whom proof was established through a warner will receive no leniency; but those who died or were killed before reaching maturity, their matter will be relatively gentler.

Ayah 38

Two things are clarified by this. First, that Allah Almighty is the Knower of all secrets of the heavens and the earth; therefore, let no one consider these warnings as mere threats, but rather these are realities which will stand revealed before everyone one day.

Second, those who will plead that if they are granted respite again they will do good, God knows their lie as well. If such people are granted respite again, they will do only what they have been doing until now.

8. Subsequent Theme: Verses 39–45

In the subsequent verses, there is a threat to the Arab polytheists: 'You are not the first nation in this land, but rather the successors of previous nations, so take warning from their states and their end. If they were destroyed for the crime of denying the messengers despite being greater than you in strength and power, then by committing the same crime, how will you escape from the wrath and anger of God? The law of God is the same for every nation; there can be no change in it. Remember that the heavens and the earth are not held by your deities, but are held by God alone. If God were to disrupt them, no one else could manage them. If, despite your rebellion, God has granted you respite, do not remain in any misconception; God does not hasten in seizing, but when He seizes, no one can flee from Him.'

In this connection, it is also recalled that before this, they used to make oaths and promises that if any messenger was sent among them, they would become the most guided nation of the world. But when the messenger of truth came, their aversion only increased. They should remember that in the plots hatched against the truth, only those people are trapped who weave the web of conspiracies.

If you reflect, in this passage, which holds the position of the conclusion of the Surah, the rhymes (fawasil) have changed. This sudden change in rhymes is a proof of the change in the speaker's tone, and the objective of this change is to draw the addressee's attention from a new perspective. Recite the verses.

9. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 39

This is a warning to the Quraysh—that the authority you enjoy in this land today is neither created by you, nor are you the first to hold authority in this country; rather, nations have already passed before you whom God destroyed as a retribution for their disbelief and arrogance and established you in their place. So if you adopt the same way, be prepared to face the same end that came before them. The scale of justice which God has established for the removal and establishment of nations makes its decision entirely impartially for every nation. You will also repeat the same history of the nations whose stories have been narrated to you, if you follow in their footsteps. This theme has also been explained in detail under verse 165 of Surah Al-An'am and verse 100 of Surah Al-A'raf.

'And whoever disbelieves - upon him is his disbelief...': That is, remember also that from anyone's disbelief God loses nothing; rather, its consequence is faced by the disbeliever themselves. God is not in need of anyone; rather, people themselves are in need of Him. If they disbelieve, instead of harming God, they will only increase His wrath, and this will become the cause of their own destruction in this world and the Hereafter.

Ayah 40

Attention is drawn to the insignificance of their partners—implying: are you sitting complacently regarding God's grasp relying on them! Look at them, what is their reality! 'Ara'aytum' (Have you considered) in such places comes to express wonder and contempt. It states: 'If they have created anything of the earth, show it to me. Or if they have any share in the heavens, tell me.'

'Or have We given them a book...': In this clause, the style of discourse changes from the second person to the third person, which further increases the expression of wonder and aversion. It states: when their partnership is not proven in any way in the heavens and the earth, then on what basis of proof have they made them partners in God's divinity? Have We given them a book in which We accepted that such and such are partners in Our divinity, and on whose authority they have made them Our partners!

'Rather, the wrongdoers do not promise each other except delusion.' That is: none of these things is present. Neither do they possess any proof, nor any authority. Rather, these people who wrong their own souls are merely showing green pastures to one another without any proof—claiming that their such and such idol possesses such and such powers, and that such and such blessings are achieved through their worship. All these statements of theirs are mere deception in which their leaders have entangled them.

'Indeed, Allah holds the heavens and the earth, lest they cease. And if they should cease, no one could hold them after Him. Indeed, He is Forbearing and Forgiving.' (41)

That is, if they are in the misconception that the deities they assume will save them or are saving them from the punishment of which they are being warned, they must remember that God alone keeps the heavens and the earth in their orbits. If He were to loosen His hold even slightly, no one else has the strength to hold them. Then, in the blink of an eye, the entire system would be disrupted.

'Indeed, He is Forbearing and Forgiving': That is, despite people's rebellion and arrogance, the respite God grants them is because He is Forbearing and Forgiving. He does not hasten in showing His wrath, but despite people's crimes, He overlooks them and grants them respite so that whoever wishes to reform themselves may do so.

Ayah 42

Since the Arabs were unlettered, i.e., unfamiliar with scripture and Shariah, the People of the Book—especially the Jews—considered them contemptible. To the extent that their scholars had issued a decree that there was no harm for them in usurping the trusts of these unlettered ones and taking interest from them. We have already quoted these self-made decrees under the verse: 'There is no blame upon us concerning the unlettered.' When the polytheists heard such statements from the Jews, naturally a kind of inferiority complex arose within them, and in response, they would make solemn oaths with great pomp: 'If any messenger is sent among us, we will show ourselves to be more guided than all other nations.'

It should be clear here that in the prophecies in previous scriptures regarding the advent of the final messenger, as we have clarified in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf, it is present in highly explicit terms that this messenger's advent will be among the Children of Ismail (the unlettered). Although the Jewish scholars concealed this, there were scholars among them—particularly among the Christians—who were waiting for the advent of a messenger among the unlettered. Thus, the Qur'an has referred to this in highly beautiful terms in the verse: 'And when they hear what has been revealed...' (Al-Ma'idah: 83). Through such scholars, this tradition was also present among the Children of Ismail that a messenger was about to be sent among them, and they expressed this against the Jews: 'If a messenger is sent among us, we will not prove weak and fragile like you, but will excel everyone in accepting guidance.' Reminding them of this statement of theirs, the Qur'an expresses regret over their state—showing that previously they made tall claims of supporting and helping the messenger to come, but when he arrived, instead of increasing in guidance, they increased in hatred and aversion. This attitude of theirs is also mentioned in Surah As-Saffat:

'And indeed, they used to say, "If we had with us a scripture from the former peoples, we would have been the devoted servants of Allah." But they disbelieved in it, so they are going to know.' (As-Saffat: 167–170)

Ayah 43

This describes the cause of their aversion to the messenger—showing that its cause is only arrogance. The truth is well clear to them, but they are not ready at any cost to sacrifice the leadership and authority they enjoy in the land; for this reason, they are opposing this manifest truth. They fear that if they acknowledge the messengership of Muhammad (pbuh), their authority will end.

'and plotting of evil' is conjoined with 'aversion' (nufoora). That is: the messenger of whose guidance they claimed they would be the flag-bearers, when he arrived, they stood up as his enemies and became active in vile conspiracies against him to harm him. Since the word 'makr' (plot) also comes in a good sense, the restriction of 'sayyi' (evil) was placed with it to clarify the nature of their plot. Above, under verse 10, the mention of their evil plots and their end has already passed.

'but the evil plot does not encompass except its authors.' Whoever conspires against the truth, in reality, conspires against themselves. Apparently, they weave this web for another, but they are the first to get trapped in this web themselves. Opposing the one who shows the correct path is to lose one's own path and ruin one's own Hereafter. They cannot bring any harm to others with this conspiracy without Allah's permission, but they deliver themselves to destruction in any case.

'Then do they await except the way of the former peoples?' 'Yandhuroon' here is in the sense of awaiting, and within 'sunnatal-awwaleen' (way of the former peoples), according to the well-known rules of Arabic, some words are omitted. If they are spelled out, the complete statement would be: 'Then do they await except Allah's way with the former peoples?'—meaning: if these people, due to their arrogance and aversion to truth, are now dedicated only to opposition and conspiracy, it means they are now waiting for that Sunnah of Allah which appeared for previous nations to appear for them as well, and for them to be destroyed in the same way those nations were destroyed for the crime of denying the messengers.

'And you will never find in the way of Allah any change, and you will never find in the way of Allah any alteration.' This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh) in highly powerful terms—that if these people are waiting for it, then rest assured that the Sunnah of Allah neither changes nor alters. 'No change' means that it is entirely identical and impartial for every nation; it is impossible that an attitude which became a cause of destruction for one nation is adopted by another and they escape from that end. 'No alteration' means that when the time for its manifestation comes, it is bound to happen. Then, altering it or changing its direction is beyond the power of anyone.

Ayah 44

'Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those before them? And they were greater than them in power.'

This is an expression of favor upon these opponents, and an expression of regret over their disregard and deprivation. It states: these have been unlettered people, unaware of the Qur'an and scripture. Before this, neither had Allah given them any book which would serve as a source of teaching, study, and guidance for them, nor had any warner come to them before you to wake them from this sleep of negligence and warn them of the dangers ahead. The requirement of this situation was that they should have valued this great blessing with heart and soul, accepted it immediately, and with it reformed their world and also attained the kingdom of the Hereafter. But they are opposing it, and in this way, inviting that wrath of God which, according to the divine law, necessarily comes upon a nation that denies the warner sent to it.

After 'Kutub' (scriptures), the attribute 'yadrusunaha' (which they could study) points to the reality that until now they had not been given heavenly scriptures of the kind given to the Children of Israel, which they would study. The teaching they had received through Hazrat Ibrahim and Hazrat Ismail (peace be upon them) had taken the form of traditions, which with the passage of time had been almost forgotten, and the dominance of innovations had completely distorted their form. Allah Almighty willed to complete His favor upon them by giving them His Book, but their condition is such that they are calling it magic and fabrication.

Ayah 45

This is a warning to them—that denying God's messenger and His warner logically results in the destruction of that nation, and the entire human history testifies to this divine law.

'and they have not attained a tenth of what We had given them': Within the discourse, this is a highly fitting warning—that reference is being made to those nations who, in terms of pomp, glory, power, and abundance of resources, had reached a position that these rebels of the Quraysh do not reach even the dust of their feet. Yet, when they denied Allah's messengers, how God's curse fell upon them has been described in detail in the Qur'an. The meaning is: if they are proud of their resources, this pride is entirely baseless. Nations of great power and glory were blown away in a single breath of God, so what is their reality? They have not attained even a tenth of what those possessed. They have gone astray over very little due to their narrow-mindedness.

By the grace and tawfeeq of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. All praise belongs to Allah for this.

Rahman Abad: 4 February, 1975 AD.
